title: Ventilation — Basics for FY1
oneLiner: Invasive/non-invasive ventilatory support for acute respiratory failure (type 1 or 2 hypoxia/hypercapnia) – recognise need, trouble-shoot at bedside, escalate before decompensation.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Circuit disconnection or obstruction (ventilator alarm, apnoea)**: single finding – no chest rise or no breath sounds despite ventilator cycling → immediate hand-bag, call anaesthetics/ICU.
- **Tension pneumothorax (especially on positive pressure)**: single finding – sudden hypotension + absent breath sounds + tracheal deviation → immediate needle decompression (2nd ICS midclavicular line, 14G cannula).
- **Severe hypotension from positive pressure (reduced preload)**: single finding – BP drop >20 mmHg after initiating/increasing PEEP or pressure support → call senior; consider fluid bolus, reduce PEEP if tolerated, rule out tension.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Dyspnoea at rest, tachypnoea, accessory muscle use, paradoxical breathing, inability to speak full sentences
  - Altered consciousness (rising PaCO₂ or hypoxia)
  - Cyanosis, diaphoresis, fatigue (pre-arrest)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Respiratory rate >25/min or <8/min
  - Auscultation: silent chest (asthma), crackles (oedema/pneumonia), absent breath sounds (pneumothorax/effusion)
  - Abdominal movement in NIV – gastric distension
  - Check ET tube depth (lips: 21–23 cm men, 20–22 cm women) and cuff palpation in neck
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Type 1 respiratory failure (hypoxia only):** pneumonia → fine crackles, focal chest signs; pulmonary oedema → raised JVP, S3 gallop, bibasal crackles; PE → sudden onset, raised D-dimer, Wells criteria; ARDS → bilateral infiltrates, no LV failure
- **Type 2 respiratory failure (hypercapnia + hypoxia):** COPD exacerbation → prolonged expiration, wheeze; opioid/barbiturate overdose → pinpoint pupils, low GCS; neuromuscular (GBS, myasthenia) → ascending weakness, ptosis; severe asthma → quiet chest, PEFR <33%
- **Splitter:** PaCO₂ + pH – if pH <7.25 and rising PaCO₂ despite maximal medical therapy → need ventilatory support regardless of aetiology
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - SpO₂ continuous, arterial blood gas (ABG) – pH, PaCO₂, PaO₂, HCO₃⁻, lactate
  - End-tidal CO₂ capnography if intubated (waveform confirms tube position + adequacy)
  - Chest x-ray (CXR) – only if safe: rule out pneumothorax, ET tube position, consolidation
  - Neurological assessment (GCS) – hypersomnolence = hypercapnic encephalopathy
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, CRP, blood cultures, troponin (if cardiac suspected)
  - D-dimer if PE suspicion (caution in high pre-test probability)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** CXR (AP erect)
  - **Second-line:** CT chest if ARDS, PE, or complex infection
- **Special tests:**
  - Spirometry (pre-morbid) for COPD/asthma – not in acute
- **Scoring/Diagnostic criteria:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 triggers escalation
  - ROX index (SpO₂/FiO₂ ÷ RR) for NIV failure prediction – if <4.88 after 2h, consider intubation
- **Never-miss test:** **ABG** before starting ventilation – you cannot manage CO₂ without baseline pH and PaCO₂.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Type 1 hypoxia: high-flow O₂ via non-rebreather (15 L/min) → if inadequate, escalate to CPAP (for cardiogenic oedema) or NIV (for acute hypoxemic failure).
  - Type 2 (COPD overlap): start NIV (BiPAP) – IPAP 10–20 cmH₂O, EPAP 4–6 cmH₂O, FiO₂ target SpO₂ 88–92% (BTS). Initiate medical therapy (nebulised bronchodilators, steroids)
  - If NIV fails (pH <7.25, rising PaCO₂, GCS <12, haemodynamic instability) → call senior/anaesthetics for intubation
  - After intubation: confirm tube position (end-tidal CO₂ + CXR), set initial settings: Volume Assist-Control (VAC), tidal volume 6 mL/kg ideal body weight, RR 12–16, PEEP 5 cmH₂O, FiO₂ 1.0 then wean quickly
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - For NIV: hourly monitoring of RR, SpO₂, ABG at 1h and 4h; adjust IPAP/EPAP to target pH >7.30, PaCO₂ decrease; if no improvement at 4h → escalate
  - For intubated: daily CXR, ABG; maintain lung-protective ventilation (Plateau pressure <30 cmH₂O); sedation holds (daily interruption); VAP bundle (head-up 30°, oral care, chlorhexidine)
  - Weaning: spontaneous breathing trial daily (e.g. T-piece or PSV) once cause resolved
- **Long-term/outpatient:**
  - Home NIV for chronic hypercapnic COPD or neuromuscular disease – refer to respiratory team
- **Default orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: 1L crystalloid over 8h unless hypotensive (then bolus 250–500 mL) – [check local protocol]
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily (or tinzaparin) – adjust for weight/renal function
  - NBM if intubated (nil by mouth except small sips for spurious cough); may allow oral intake during NIV if stable
  - Observations: continuous SpO₂ + RR + MAP every 15 min first hour, then hourly; ABG at 1h and 4h after NIV start
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **NIV adjunct:** No routine sedation; if anxious (rare) – [check local policy] e.g. haloperidol 1–2 mg PO/IV PRN
- **Intubated sedation:** Propofol (titrate 1–4 mg/kg/h) or midazolam 2–10 mg/h IV – [check local policy]; avoid bolus in hypotension
- **Analgesia:** Morphine 1–5 mg IV PRN (caution in renal failure, hypotension)
- **Paralysis (if needed for severe ARDS):** Rocuronium 0.6 mg/kg IV bolus then 8–12 μg/kg/min – **only in ICU**; reversal with sugammadex
- **Key contraindications:**
  - NIV – facial trauma, recent oesophageal surgery, uncooperative patient, vomiting risk (consider NGT)
  - Propofol – egg/soya allergy, high triglycerides
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Morphine – reduce dose in CKD (GFR <30: 50% dose) – risk of accumulation
  - Midazolam – reduce in hepatic impairment (50% dose)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **NIV:** Applying EPAP >10 cmH₂O without checking blood pressure – can cause hypotension
  - **Intubation:** Using succinylcholine in hyperkalaemia (e.g. chronic dialysis, burns) – use rocuronium instead
  - **Sedation:** Over-sedating a hypotensive patient with propofol bolus – gives cardiovascular collapse
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - NIV: gastric distension, aspiration, pressure sores from mask
  - Invasive: ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP), barotrauma (pneumothorax), ventilator asynchrony, self-extubation, tracheal stenosis (prolonged)
- **Chronic:**
  - Ventilator dependence (failure to wean), muscle atrophy, long-term NIV dependence
sections.Prognosis:
- **NIV in acute COPD:** ~80–90% avoid intubation, overall hospital mortality ~10–15% (BTS data)
- **Invasive ventilation overall:** ~30–50% ICU mortality depending on severity (higher in ARDS, sepsis)
- **Successful weaning:** ~60–70% at 14 days; prolonged >7 days associated with poor outcome
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **DO** perform daily spontaneous breathing trial (SBT) in stable patients – reduces days on vent
- **DO** cap capnography waveform – a flat waveform after intubation = oesophageal intubation until proved otherwise (immediate extubate and reattempt)
- **DO** set FiO₂ to target SpO₂ 88–92% (COPD) or 94–98% (other) – don't chase 100% without checking CO₂ retention
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **DO NOT** assume SpO₂ normal means adequate ventilation – a patient can have PaCO₂ >10 kPa and SpO₂ 95% on O₂
- **DO NOT** delay intubation waiting for better ABG – if GCS <12, pH <7.25, rising PaCO₂ despite NIV, call for help
- **DO NOT** apply NIV to patient with vomiting risk without NGT – aspiration is lethal
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Increasing breathlessness at rest, unable to sleep flat, or new confusion
  - Fever >38°C or productive purulent sputum
  - Home O₂ requirement increased >2 L/min above baseline
- **Follow-up:**
  - Respiratory clinic within 2–4 weeks for weaning/coping with home NIV
  - Pulmonary rehab or smoking cessation referral
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior/ICU if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or sustained rise >2 points despite treatment
  - PaCO₂ rising (pH <7.25) on NIV at 4h
  - GCS <12 or fall >2 points
  - Systolic BP <90 mmHg or lactate >4 mmol/L
  - Tension pneumothorax or circuit disconnection unresponsive to simple correction
- **Call crash team (2222):**
  - Cardiac arrest, apnoea, unresponsive airway emergency
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - **NIV failure:** pH <7.25 + rising PaCO₂ at 4h = urgent intubation
  - **Intubated:** SpO₂ <88% on FiO₂ >0.6, plateau pressure >30 cmH₂O, PEEP >15 cmH₂O – escalate to ICU consultant

rawMarkdown:
## Ventilation — Basics for FY1
**One-liner:** Invasive/non-invasive ventilatory support for acute respiratory failure (type 1 or 2 hypoxia/hypercapnia) – recognise need, trouble-shoot at bedside, escalate before decompensation.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Circuit disconnection or obstruction (ventilator alarm, apnoea)**: single finding – no chest rise or no breath sounds despite ventilator cycling → immediate hand-bag, call anaesthetics/ICU.
- **Tension pneumothorax (especially on positive pressure)**: single finding – sudden hypotension + absent breath sounds + tracheal deviation → immediate needle decompression (2nd ICS midclavicular line, 14G cannula).
- **Severe hypotension from positive pressure (reduced preload)**: single finding – BP drop >20 mmHg after initiating/increasing PEEP or pressure support → call senior; consider fluid bolus, reduce PEEP if tolerated, rule out tension.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Dyspnoea at rest, tachypnoea, accessory muscle use, paradoxical breathing, inability to speak full sentences
  - Altered consciousness (rising PaCO₂ or hypoxia)
  - Cyanosis, diaphoresis, fatigue (pre-arrest)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Respiratory rate >25/min or <8/min
  - Auscultation: silent chest (asthma), crackles (oedema/pneumonia), absent breath sounds (pneumothorax/effusion)
  - Abdominal movement in NIV – gastric distension
  - Check ET tube depth (lips: 21–23 cm men, 20–22 cm women) and cuff palpation in neck

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Type 1 respiratory failure (hypoxia only):** pneumonia → fine crackles, focal chest signs; pulmonary oedema → raised JVP, S3 gallop, bibasal crackles; PE → sudden onset, raised D-dimer, Wells criteria; ARDS → bilateral infiltrates, no LV failure
- **Type 2 respiratory failure (hypercapnia + hypoxia):** COPD exacerbation → prolonged expiration, wheeze; opioid/barbiturate overdose → pinpoint pupils, low GCS; neuromuscular (GBS, myasthenia) → ascending weakness, ptosis; severe asthma → quiet chest, PEFR <33%
- **Splitter:** PaCO₂ + pH – if pH <7.25 and rising PaCO₂ despite maximal medical therapy → need ventilatory support regardless of aetiology

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - SpO₂ continuous, arterial blood gas (ABG) – pH, PaCO₂, PaO₂, HCO₃⁻, lactate
  - End-tidal CO₂ capnography if intubated (waveform confirms tube position + adequacy)
  - Chest x-ray (CXR) – only if safe: rule out pneumothorax, ET tube position, consolidation
  - Neurological assessment (GCS) – hypersomnolence = hypercapnic encephalopathy
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, CRP, blood cultures, troponin (if cardiac suspected)
  - D-dimer if PE suspicion (caution in high pre-test probability)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** CXR (AP erect)
  - **Second-line:** CT chest if ARDS, PE, or complex infection
- **Special tests:**
  - Spirometry (pre-morbid) for COPD/asthma – not in acute
- **Scoring/Diagnostic criteria:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 triggers escalation
  - ROX index (SpO₂/FiO₂ ÷ RR) for NIV failure prediction – if <4.88 after 2h, consider intubation
- **Never-miss test:** **ABG** before starting ventilation – you cannot manage CO₂ without baseline pH and PaCO₂.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Type 1 hypoxia: high-flow O₂ via non-rebreather (15 L/min) → if inadequate, escalate to CPAP (for cardiogenic oedema) or NIV (for acute hypoxemic failure).
  - Type 2 (COPD overlap): start NIV (BiPAP) – IPAP 10–20 cmH₂O, EPAP 4–6 cmH₂O, FiO₂ target SpO₂ 88–92% (BTS). Initiate medical therapy (nebulised bronchodilators, steroids)
  - If NIV fails (pH <7.25, rising PaCO₂, GCS <12, haemodynamic instability) → call senior/anaesthetics for intubation
  - After intubation: confirm tube position (end-tidal CO₂ + CXR), set initial settings: Volume Assist-Control (VAC), tidal volume 6 mL/kg ideal body weight, RR 12–16, PEEP 5 cmH₂O, FiO₂ 1.0 then wean quickly
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - For NIV: hourly monitoring of RR, SpO₂, ABG at 1h and 4h; adjust IPAP/EPAP to target pH >7.30, PaCO₂ decrease; if no improvement at 4h → escalate
  - For intubated: daily CXR, ABG; maintain lung-protective ventilation (Plateau pressure <30 cmH₂O); sedation holds (daily interruption); VAP bundle (head-up 30°, oral care, chlorhexidine)
  - Weaning: spontaneous breathing trial daily (e.g. T-piece or PSV) once cause resolved
- **Long-term/outpatient:**
  - Home NIV for chronic hypercapnic COPD or neuromuscular disease – refer to respiratory team
- **Default orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: 1L crystalloid over 8h unless hypotensive (then bolus 250–500 mL) – [check local protocol]
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily (or tinzaparin) – adjust for weight/renal function
  - NBM if intubated (nil by mouth except small sips for spurious cough); may allow oral intake during NIV if stable
  - Observations: continuous SpO₂ + RR + MAP every 15 min first hour, then hourly; ABG at 1h and 4h after NIV start

### Drugs & Doses
- **NIV adjunct:** No routine sedation; if anxious (rare) – [check local policy] e.g. haloperidol 1–2 mg PO/IV PRN
- **Intubated sedation:** Propofol (titrate 1–4 mg/kg/h) or midazolam 2–10 mg/h IV – [check local policy]; avoid bolus in hypotension
- **Analgesia:** Morphine 1–5 mg IV PRN (caution in renal failure, hypotension)
- **Paralysis (if needed for severe ARDS):** Rocuronium 0.6 mg/kg IV bolus then 8–12 μg/kg/min – **only in ICU**; reversal with sugammadex
- **Key contraindications:**
  - NIV – facial trauma, recent oesophageal surgery, uncooperative patient, vomiting risk (consider NGT)
  - Propofol – egg/soya allergy, high triglycerides
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Morphine – reduce dose in CKD (GFR <30: 50% dose) – risk of accumulation
  - Midazolam – reduce in hepatic impairment (50% dose)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **NIV:** Applying EPAP >10 cmH₂O without checking blood pressure – can cause hypotension
  - **Intubation:** Using succinylcholine in hyperkalaemia (e.g. chronic dialysis, burns) – use rocuronium instead
  - **Sedation:** Over-sedating a hypotensive patient with propofol bolus – gives cardiovascular collapse

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - NIV: gastric distension, aspiration, pressure sores from mask
  - Invasive: ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP), barotrauma (pneumothorax), ventilator asynchrony, self-extubation, tracheal stenosis (prolonged)
- **Chronic:**
  - Ventilator dependence (failure to wean), muscle atrophy, long-term NIV dependence

### Prognosis
- **NIV in acute COPD:** ~80–90% avoid intubation, overall hospital mortality ~10–15% (BTS data)
- **Invasive ventilation overall:** ~30–50% ICU mortality depending on severity (higher in ARDS, sepsis)
- **Successful weaning:** ~60–70% at 14 days; prolonged >7 days associated with poor outcome

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **DO** perform daily spontaneous breathing trial (SBT) in stable patients – reduces days on vent
- **DO** cap capnography waveform – a flat waveform after intubation = oesophageal intubation until proved otherwise (immediate extubate and reattempt)
- **DO** set FiO₂ to target SpO₂ 88–92% (COPD) or 94–98% (other) – don't chase 100% without checking CO₂ retention

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **DO NOT** assume SpO₂ normal means adequate ventilation – a patient can have PaCO₂ >10 kPa and SpO₂ 95% on O₂
- **DO NOT** delay intubation waiting for better ABG – if GCS <12, pH <7.25, rising PaCO₂ despite NIV, call for help
- **DO NOT** apply NIV to patient with vomiting risk without NGT – aspiration is lethal

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Increasing breathlessness at rest, unable to sleep flat, or new confusion
  - Fever >38°C or productive purulent sputum
  - Home O₂ requirement increased >2 L/min above baseline
- **Follow-up:**
  - Respiratory clinic within 2–4 weeks for weaning/coping with home NIV
  - Pulmonary rehab or smoking cessation referral

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior/ICU if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or sustained rise >2 points despite treatment
  - PaCO₂ rising (pH <7.25) on NIV at 4h
  - GCS <12 or fall >2 points
  - Systolic BP <90 mmHg or lactate >4 mmol/L
  - Tension pneumothorax or circuit disconnection unresponsive to simple correction
- **Call crash team (2222):**
  - Cardiac arrest, apnoea, unresponsive airway emergency
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - **NIV failure:** pH <7.25 + rising PaCO₂ at 4h = urgent intubation
  - **Intubated:** SpO₂ <88% on FiO₂ >0.6, plateau pressure >30 cmH₂O, PEEP >15 cmH₂O – escalate to ICU consultant
